groups.text: It is currently a clear morning in Berlin-Tempelhof with a temperature of 35 degrees.
With the morning weather set, here is the news.

A rare genetic mutation can strike even those most dedicated to their health, as seen with founder Connor Christou. Despite tracking nearly 100 biomarkers annually and using wearables like Oura and Whoop to optimize his life, Christou was diagnosed with an aggressive form of non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma after a mass was found behind his sternum. To fight the disease, he fed his blood results, scan data, and journal entries into the AI model Claude. This intersection of personal health and machine intelligence mirrors broader shifts in how we use AI to solve complex problems. For instance, Sina recently released VibeThinker-3B, a small language model that uses multi-stage post-training on an Alibaba base. Despite having only three billion parameters, it matches models up to 333 times its size, like DeepSeek V3.2, in math and coding. However, researchers found that while logical reasoning compresses well, broad factual knowledge still requires much larger models.

Author Margaret Atwood is sounding an alarm on the reliability of artificial intelligence. Speaking at the Babell Literary and Cultural Festival in Porto, Portugal, the writer of The Handmaid's Tale described the fundamental problem with AI as garbage in, garbage out. Atwood shared her own experience using Anthropic's Claude chatbot to find information about the British detective series Father Brown. She says the model provided the wrong answer or lied, though she notes a machine cannot consciously lie because it is not human. For Atwood, this failure highlights the limitations of large language models that simply skim data rather than understand truth. Her critique suggests that these tools often prioritize a plausible response over an accurate one, leaving users to navigate a landscape of digital hallucinations.

The race for AI supremacy is shifting toward strategic deterrence, as Chinese cybersecurity firm 360 Security Technology unveils two new tools to rival Anthropic's Mythos. Founder Zhou Hongyi describes these automated vulnerability and defense systems, named Tu Long Feng and Yi Tian Zhen, as a necessary deterrent against what he calls cyber nuclear weapons. While one tool has already flagged over three thousand vulnerabilities, Zhou admits China's top models still trail Western systems by 20 to 30 percent. To bridge this gap, researchers are experimenting with new architectures. ByteDance and Renmin University recently released iLLaDA, an 8-billion parameter diffusion language model. Unlike the autoregressive method used by ChatGPT, iLLaDA refines text in parallel from masked tokens. While it matches Qwen2.5 at a base level, it falls behind after fine-tuning, highlighting the ongoing struggle to match Western quality through alternative technical paths.

The companies most likely to automate your job are now funding the program meant to retrain you. Former US Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo has launched a bipartisan nonprofit called Raise Us to prepare American workers for an AI-driven economy. The organization aims to raise one billion dollars for continuing education and retraining, with half of that amount already secured. This effort is backed by more than two dozen major corporations and four governors, including funding from Amazon, Anthropic, Microsoft, and the OpenAI Foundation. Raimondo, who will serve as CEO alongside former Indiana Governor Eric Holcomb, argues that America lacks a people strategy to match its technology strategy. She warns that if the country builds the best AI systems but leaves millions of workers behind, it will have simply automated its own decline. The nonprofit now plans to create new corporate incentives for retention and launch pilot programs with governors.

The tension over how to scale artificial intelligence is moving from the ground to the stars. Elon Musk has proposed building orbital data centers, but SoftBank CEO Masayoshi Son is skeptical. Son argues that space-based centers will take too long to build and won't effectively cut costs during a critical few years of AI competition. Some critics suggest the vision primarily serves to guarantee more business for SpaceX by requiring constant satellite replacements. While the infrastructure debate continues, users are already feeling the impact on their daily routines. A survey of roughly 9,700 Claude users by Anthropic found that half say AI can already handle 50 percent or more of their work tasks. This is most evident in database queries and marketing content. Looking ahead, 26 percent of those users expect AI to cover between 60 and 90 percent of their workload within the next twelve months.

OpenAI is strengthening its hardware ambitions by recruiting Paul Meade from Apple. Meade served as the vice president in charge of the Vision Pro headset and reportedly led the development of the AI-powered smart glasses Apple plans to launch next year. This departure follows a shakeup of Apple's hardware engineering team under John Ternus, who is expected to be elevated to CEO. Some vice presidents felt demoted during this restructuring, creating an opening for OpenAI to bring Meade on board. He joins a team already collaborating with former Apple design chief Jony Ive on a new AI device. Sam Altman has claimed this upcoming product will be more peaceful and calm than an iPhone, though reports from last fall suggest the company has struggled to get the technical details right. While the costly Vision Pro failed to become a hit, Apple is now banking on more affordable glasses to compete with Meta.

Apple is raising prices across its hardware lineup, with Tim Cook calling the increases unavoidable and describing previous pricing as unsustainable. The 16-inch MacBook Pro has jumped by 300 dollars, while the 11-inch iPad Air rose from 599 to 749 dollars, and even the HomePod Mini saw a 30 dollar increase. Cook attributes these costs to the AI industry, a trend already affecting gaming consoles like the Xbox. While U.S. companies face these pressures, geopolitical restrictions are shifting the market in Asia. The Trump administration recently banned Anthropic’s powerful Mythos and Fable 5 models from non-American users. In response, Chinese firm 360 unveiled Tulongfeng to compete with Mythos, and Tokyo-based Sakana AI launched a frontier model called Fugu. Sakana is now capitalizing on the U.S. export ban by advertising that its new model delivers high-level capability without the risk of government controls.
After the headlines, back to the weather.
This afternoon will be cloudy with rain chances up to 65 percent and temperatures between 29 and 39 degrees.
And that's it for your personal morning broadcast.
[mature adult voice, intensely emotional, tender, radiant; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
[very very long pause before speaking; then mature adult voice, most emotional, strong, warm, radiant, deeply encouraging; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
